reviews.review_msg: Nice guy. Took this new course with him. Overall was a good professor but there was enough feedback quickly enough. Overall, if your grades don't matter and you just want a enjoyable class go with Terry.

I was in Terry's first Douglas class, and he's a super nice man! Super understanding, accommodating, and well spoken. The class is easy, just reflective journals and one bigger group project that you choose the topic for. The only negative is he doesn't follow structures, so you have a lot of freedom, which leads to confusion sometimes.
Best prof I've ever had. Cares for students and teaches students to think outside the box.
If you need a concrete guideline for assignments to get them done, this class isn't for you. He's vague about EVERYTHING, including his expectations. Easy marker though, and cares for his students.
does not undermine, encourages, best prof currently at sfu
Class is lecture heavy. Personally, I found it difficult to connect the lectures to the course readings. Speaks awfully slow and has stale humor. Assignments and exam are vague.

I've had Terry for 3 classes in my UD. He's a chill prof, tests are open-book and application based but not difficult. His classes are lecture and paper heavy but once again, not difficult. He is interesting, knowledgeable about the topic, and often shares stories. CMNS 447 was easy, it was dialogue based so all we did was talk to each other.

Terry was awesome. Really caring, fair, gave good feedback, and was entertaining. I personally really liked the lecture style, but it's definitely different. You'll want to do the readings and participate. As long as you do you'll do well.

Terry is funny and inspiring. His lecture-style can be a little bit eccentric, but I thought that was appropriate given how the entire course is based on the premise of getting you to think outside the box. I'd take Terry any day over a standard, read-off-the-powerpoint professor.

Terry is a nice guy and he is willing to take a look at paper drafts and tell you where you can improve.  That said, I couldn't really follow his lecture style as he presented a new, unrelated topic each week (medical discourse, religious discourse, etc) which was very different than what I expected from the course title.

Terry brings his real life day job as a mediator to the class room which gives the material a lot more depth. Do the work, participate, go to class, follow the criteria, and think outside the box and you'll get a good mark. He's also always willing to help in office hours if you need it. Great prof! Take a class with him if you can!

He's bit eccentric, and his outside-the-box approach to the course left a few people scratching their heads (but those people could use a little shaking up.)  Academia needs more people like this who aren't just spewing the standard fare to be repeated back to them on exams. If you can get over his slight creepy uncle vibe, you'll be glad you came.

Terry was just OK in my opinion. He seems like a genuinely really nice guy who is unquestionably passionate about what he teaches, but I honestly found the whole concept of the course a bit of a stretch. Most of the time I felt like he was just making stuff up. I ended up with a good grade but can confidently say that I have no idea what I learned.

Your grade for 85% of the class is based on a group project that spans the entire semester, which is great as long as you choose wisely who will be in your group (which you decide in the 2nd week). Not much direction is given for the projects, which means if you don't ask you're likely not going to do well. Be prepared to work.

Terry is a great prof, who appears knowledgeable and passionate about the content he teaches. Assignments are fair, and the in-class essay was an open-book test. Overall an interesting class and I enjoyed the lectures.

I loved Terry's class. He really teaches you how to think outside the box and is a very brilliant & funny professor. His exams were also all open book/laptop/textbook but the questions were very abstract. I would definitely rec. a class w/ him if you want to open your perspective.

He's very helpful. He can be reached easily when you need a help. He even left us his phone number. However, I feel like his lectures were flat and boring. His expectations for assignments were unclear as well.

I do not understand why Terry's mark is so low. He is brillant, funny and interresting. I agree three hours lecture is intense but if you participate and do the readings, you will enjoy his lectures. I intend to take more courses with him, if I can.

My favorite professor and class ive ever taken at SFU. The epitome of a professor who teaches you HOW to think, not WHAT to think. He has a wit to him, and is a very articulate speaker who really is interested in what he teaches. I learned a lot. But not in a textbook way. He relates things to real life quite often to make things a lot better too.
Cares about his students. Final exam is easy. Lectures can feel a little long, but are pretty interesting.

This class puzzled me at first, and I felt very lost- but I think I know why. In many CMNS courses, you are expected to regurgitate, and that is what many CMNS students are trained to do. This course's content, however, is very much open to interpretation and you take away your own meaning. I think you should take this class, don't be scared off.
What an amazing instructor. This here is a professor who cares about his students.
Neiman has an interesting background and we're lucky to have him teaching us!

He's such an amazing guy that I would chill with him for fun. Regardless of what grade you get, this is the most genuinely interesting class I've taken at SFU. I'd recommend it for anyone interested in language, dialogue & the human experience.
like his way!

I was really excited for this class, until i got the "student manual" which is what he calls the class syllabus. In it is the only real reference for the term assignment which is the focus of the entire class. The example essays he gave out went on for paragraphs like literary poems that made connections that i couldn't personally see.

Couldn't link readings to the lecture without the TAs help. Papers are given without very many guidelines, which makes it hard to know what they're looking for, resulting in lower grades because they mark hard. TA (DYLAN) WAS HORRIBLE WITH PROVIDING FEEDBACK IN A TIMELY FASHION. Get 2 TAs? Can't seem to keep up with the marking. Don't take it.
AWESOME lecturer, had me engaged the whole three hours every time. Brings familiar things like a Hendrix song or an Orson Wells film into context in a way that makes you see things differently.
good
An amazing prof. His lectures were fun and very interesting. This course is definitely worth taking.

One of the most intelligent and genuine profs in CMNS, if you are lucky enough to have him. Terry has had my full attention in every one of our 9AM lectures, and every time, I am compelled to fill my notebook with his words. If you are someone who has a brain and cares for social discourse, you will find this class very intellectually stimulating.

At first I thought I hated Terry because of his dislike of big words. (Seriously, this is an UD CMNS course...) But it turns out I've loved this class. He's a great lecturer & really wants people to learn. You MUST do the readings & keep up with the course. Exams are actually based on learning & understanding, not rote memorisation-what a concept!!
Good prof. Quite difficult but him and TA are willing to help. You learn a lot new concepts in this class.

Terry teaches concepts. You couldn't cheat in this class if you tried. If you are interested in doing graduate school then take a class from Terry.. Encyclopaedic knowledge of Social thinkers/theories from the past 200 years. The most important thing you will learn from Terry is how to make connections from abstractions and relate them to real life

I really like Terry, he is funny and engaging, he does his best to get class participation and he does lots of activities to break the long class up. He is a bit confusing in that it is not always easy to know how what we are talking about relates to the course but if you ask questions he is happy to clarify. He's a cool guy. Course is very different from what I expected, but Terry is a smart guy.

Terry comes off as a great guy. However, his lectures tend to be quite dry most of the time and the readings even drier. For my semester he decided to take attendance in lecture (wasn't a seminar), which really put me off. The class itself is interesting, but the way he presents the material could use a face-lift.

The worst class ever. Instead of list this class as communication course, I think its more like a philosophy class. The course materials are super dry and doesnt make any sense! I cant see how it connects to EVERYDAY LIFE COMMUNICATION. Anyways, do your work you will be fine. I hope someone else can teach this class.

Terry is very passionate and genuine. The course covers a lot of topics that seem unrelated but he brings them all together in a really interesting way. He can be hard to follow sometimes but always is super helpful and willing to clarify. If you engage in the course and pay attention to what he mentions in lectures you'll be golden!
Really intelligent. He is helpful, and makes an effort to make the class interesting and interactive.

Terry is fantastic. He's a great speaker and always makes an effort to make his lectures more interactive. If you're looking to take only one of 323 or 304, I would definitely choose 304, as long as Terry's still teaching it. It's a great primer for a lot of other upper division CMNS courses.
I wish someone else would teach the course :(

He doesn't explain any of his concepts clearly and expects us to understand how his lectures are related to the class when they are almost entirely separate material. He seems like a nice guy, but definitely not a nice prof and not an easy marker.

I took CMNS 200 with Terry in spring 2011. He was extremely helpful and went out of his way to ensure everyone was on track with their term projects. Gained a lot of practical skills and experience to take into the workforce.
Hands down one of the most intelligent profs I have ever come across. This guy just knows stuff about everything and made lectures very enjoyable.

Terry is a wonderful prof and an excellent T/A. He is passionate about what he is teaching and is helpful when students ask questions. This course definitely pushes you to come up with your own ideas and work a little more independently. Topics are not just handed to you on a silver platter. Welcome to upper division courses.

Terry is an awesome. Genuinely nice lecturer and seems to really care in  helping others learn. can't help the fact that the course material is a bit dry overall but it is a 3rd year course. Also, marking is fair but a bit difficult to attain 'excellent' marks.

I was not looking forward to this class from reading comments here, however, after a great semester I absolutely can't understand what everyone's problem is. Terry is a great prof, very dynamic and enthusiastic. He is clear, has good notes but also expects alot from his students - as he should, it's a 3rd yr class! Julie (TA) is awesome too!

I'm surprised at all the bad comments - it's not the most exciting class I've ever taken, that's for sure, but Terry is a nice guy and not a hard marker - good sense of humor too. Rambles a bit in lecture, but if you pay attention and do most of the readings, you're set. Seriously, this class is NOT as difficult as people are making it out to be!!

Terry was one of the best professors I've had at SFU. Some of the other comments on this thread are completely ridiculous; I didn't see half of those things in class! Terry is very fair and has a unique teaching and marking style which I thought was very helpful. He is very nice, clearly interested in the course content, and happy to offer help!

This class has nothing to do with "everyday communication" at all. I am so confused 99% of the time and I honestly never know what the relevance of what he talks about to the course is. Brutal class with so much writing. The readings are so dry as well.

The worst professor I have encountered in my four years at SFU. He is vague with his expectations but becomes extremely picky when he marks assignments. I've done three co-op workterms prior to taking this class, and I can tell you that you do NOT need this class to get a co-op. This class is a waste of money, time and energy.

Terry is by far one of the worst professors I have ever had at SFU. He is extremely vague when giving criteria for assignments, yet he is extremely critical and picky when it comes time to marking them.

There is little structure or coherence to this course. The concepts are confusing and amorphous. It would help if Terry would draw clear and strong links between the concepts and the conflicts he presents. The mid-term (worth 40%) is actually 10% test and 30% proposal for the final paper. Mid-term was "open computer" - not fair to students without

Totally boring and confusing. I tried looking up concepts he talked about and they seem non-existent. Little structure to class. Never missed a class, but not really sure what it was about. Don't take this class! It is nothing like what it sounds like it is about.

Neiman is the most unhelpful instructor SFU has hired. Avoid taking his class if at all possible. He is biased and immature. His lectures are unrelated, irrelevant, and are not tested on at all. His T.A. (scott) is just as awful. DON'T TAKE.

Neiman is the worst professor I have encountered so far at SFU. He teaches first year concepts in his 3rd year class, is biased and unhelpful. his textbook is useless, his examples are irrelevant, and his overall attitude towards students is poor. i wish i hadn't taken his class.

I read over everyones comment about Neiman. They are entirely true. He is vague with his marking. When I requested an explanation of why he gave me this grade, he babbled contradicting nonsense as if he made it up on the spot. When I tried harder, I realized my grade would not change. He obviously already decided my fate.

Terry is a sessional grad student teaching university courses because SFU couldn't find someone interested/better. gives little to no feedback, uses unrelated examples, keeps students in the dark, tell s you you're wrong then doesn't explain why. avoid his class if you can.
Super interesting classes. The class is a bit mind boggling (in a good way). Definitely take it if you like being critical of every aspect of daily life. Great class. Fantastic TA (Angela Gunther).

Passionate BUT provided minimal feedback on project proposals and then ripped the project apart at the end. Seems very set on his ways... very hard marker. Intelligent and interesting BUT an incredibly hard marker.
Terry is a prof that gives little direction to his students, he's so vague that its hard to keep up. He invents his own "theories" which he likes to refer to, but they're pretty useless
Don't understand what is trying to get at many of the times...make me have a bad impression of dialogue courses in CMNS. He can be very vague.

Harshest marker I've come across.  He'll decide off the bat what 'type' of student you are and then give you the according grade for the rest of the semester (I doubt he looks at any assignment after the 1st). He assumes that all students are 'brainless', 'overly brainless', or 'completely brainless'.  Don't expect anything better than a B.
Avoid at all cost.The most biased prof I have ever come across. He has a huge ego and loves to hear his own voice.
Terry means well but is the most confusing prof I have come across. And I know I am not the only one that is confused during lectures.

Neiman is the worst prof anyone could ever have. He loves to hear himself talk while he ignores the concerns of the students. he is not very supportive or attentive during his office hours. kinda makes you feel like you are wasting his time. his class makes no sense and neiman seems to enjoy the fact that his students are all confused.

Terry is an extremely smart guy.  This class is definately not what I thought it would be- "communication in everyday life"... the fairly complex ideas you learn seem like they couldn't be further from everyday life, but they end up making sense.
